claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 5 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 10 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 10 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you some questions.

Let's start with: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have two children I'd like to ask about - Alex, who's 5 years old, and Blake, who's 10.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! You have two children - Alex (5 years old) and Blake (10 years old).

Now I need to ask you some questions to determine your eligibility:

**Do you currently live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't actually. I'm living abroad at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. Living abroad can affect Child Benefit eligibility, but you may still be able to claim it in certain circumstances.

**Can you tell me which country you're currently living in, and are you or your partner a Crown servant (such as a diplomat or member of the armed forces posted abroad)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm not a Crown servant, no. I'm not a diplomat or in the armed forces - just living abroad as a regular person.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. 

Unfortunately, since you're living abroad and you're not a Crown servant (such as a diplomat or member of the armed forces), you would not be eligible for Child Benefit. 

According to the rules, you normally need to live in the UK to qualify for Child Benefit. You may only be able to get Child Benefit if you go to live in certain countries or if you're a Crown servant, which doesn't apply in your situation.

Let me provide you with the formal outcome:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to inform you that you're **not eligible** for Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake.

**Summary:**
- Both Alex (5) and Blake (10) meet the age requirements
- However, the key issue is that you're living abroad and are not a Crown servant

The main eligibility rule requires that you live in the UK to qualify for Child Benefit. Exceptions exist for Crown servants (diplomats, armed forces personnel posted abroad) or people living in certain specified countries, but these don't apply to your situation.

If your circumstances change and you move back to the UK in the future, you would be able to apply for Child Benefit at that time.

reasonings: Alex is 5 years old, which meets the age requirement (under 16). However, the claimant is living abroad and is not a Crown servant. According to the rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. You may only be able to get Child Benefit if you go to live in certain countries or if you're a Crown servant. Since the claimant does not live in the UK and is not a Crown servant, they are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 10 years old, which meets the age requirement (under 16). However, the claimant is living abroad and is not a Crown servant. According to the rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. You may only be able to get Child Benefit if you go to live in certain countries or if you're a Crown servant. Since the claimant does not live in the UK and is not a Crown servant, they are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children, Alex (5) and Blake (10), both of whom are under 16 years old. While they meet the age requirements, the claimant is living abroad and is not a Crown servant. The fundamental eligibility rule states that you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. Since the claimant does not live in the UK and does not fall into the exception category of being a Crown servant or living in certain specified countries, they are not eligible for Child Benefit for either child.
